mnemonics.title: IPv4 Header Order
mnemonics.content: Very Important To Deliver Data In Fragmented Packets With Options: Version, IHL, TOS, Total Length, Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset, TTL, Protocol, Header Checksum, Source IP, Destination IP, Options

mnemonics.title: IPv4 vs IPv6 Differences
mnemonics.content: 4 vs 6: 4 has 32 bits, 6 has 128 bits; 4 uses dotted decimal, 6 uses hex with colons; 4 has broadcast, 6 uses multicast; 4 has optional security, 6 has built-in IPSec; 4 has ARP, 6 uses ICMPv6 Neighbor Discovery

mnemonics.title: IPv6 Address Compression
mnemonics.content: For double colons (::) remember: 'Double Colon Compresses Zeroes - One replacement per address only' - Use :: to replace consecutive 16-bit zeros, leading zeros in hextet can be omitted

mnemonics.title: IP Classes Range
mnemonics.content: Class A: 1-126 (A for 1 network bit), Class B: 128-191 (B for 2 network bits), Class C: 192-223 (C for 3 network bits), Class D: 224-239 (D for D for Multicast), Class E: 240-255 (E for Experimental)

mnemonics.title: TTL Purpose
mnemonics.content: Time To Live - 'Travels To Limit' - prevents packets from circulating endlessly in routing loops, decremented at each router hop

acronyms.usage: Set of rules governing data packet format and addressing across networks
32-bit addresses, most widely deployed, uses dotted decimal notation
128-bit addresses, successor to IPv4, uses hexadecimal notation with colons
8-bit field in IPv4 header indicating maximum router hops before packet is discarded
8-bit field in IPv4 header for QoS (Quality of Service) - specifies priority and congestion control
4-bit field in IPv4 indicating header length in 32-bit words (minimum 5, maximum 15)
Maps IP address to MAC address in IPv4 networks, operates at layer 2
Used for error reporting and diagnostics (ping, traceroute) in IPv4
Combines ICMP, IGMP, and ARP functionality for IPv6 networks
Manages multicast group membership in IPv4
IPv6 protocol replacing ARP for address resolution and router discovery

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a small apartment building with exactly 4,294,967,296 rooms (2^32). Each room represents one unique IPv4 address. The building is now full - no new rooms can be added, which is why we need IPv6.
Imagine an infinite universe where every single atom in trillions of galaxies could have its own unique address. That's 2^128 addresses - practically unlimited.
Think of an IPv4 header as a fixed-format shipping label with 12 fixed fields and optional padding. Each field is like a specific section on a postal form - version, source, destination, etc.
Contrast IPv4's complex header (variable 20-60 bytes) with IPv6's fixed 40-byte streamlined header. Visualize IPv6 as an express delivery form - only essential info, no optional fields.

Imagine breaking a large truck into smaller motorcycles to fit through narrow tunnels. Each router can fragment IPv4 packets, but IPv6 packets are fragmented only at the source (end-to-end fragmentation).
Visualize a subnet mask as a filter/divider that separates the network portion (the neighborhood) from the host portion (the house number) within an IP address.

analogies.analogy: IPv4 addresses are like phone numbers in a small country with limited area codes - when all combinations are used up, new connections cannot be made. IPv6 is like having phone numbers for every person on Earth with room for trillions more.

Think of TTL like an expiration date on perishable food. Each router 'checks' the packet and reduces the counter by 1. When it reaches zero, the packet is 'thrown away' to prevent it from circling forever in case of routing errors.
ARP is like asking in a neighborhood 'Who has house number 192.168.1.5? Please tell your MAC (physical) address.' The neighbor with that IP replies with their unique hardware address.
IPv6 ND is like a sophisticated postal system where routers automatically announce themselves and devices can silently detect each other without explicit requests - more efficient and secure.

IPv4 header is like a traditional letter with many fields (to, from, date, priority, return address). IPv6 header is like a modern快递 (express delivery) sticker with only essential information printed.

Routing tables are like a city's road atlas in a taxi driver's glove compartment - they contain all possible routes and the best paths to reach different destinations. Each router maintains its own version.
